topics.title: SQL Basics
topics.description: SQL is the universal language for querying relational databases. Master SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, GROUP BY, and aggregate functions — these 5 clauses handle 80% of real-world data queries.
topics.story: Imagine a library with millions of books in perfectly organised shelves (tables). SQL is the language you speak to the librarian. 'Give me all books (SELECT *) written after 2000 (WHERE year > 2000) sorted by author (ORDER BY author).' The librarian (database engine) translates your English-like query into a lightning-fast lookup through the shelves.
topics.analogy: A database table is an Excel spreadsheet — rows are records, columns are fields. SQL is the formula bar that can filter, sort, and summarize any combination of those spreadsheet cells — across billions of rows — in milliseconds.
topics.memoryAnchor: SQL clause order: SELECT → FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY → LIMIT. Remember: 'Silly Frogs Will Get Hungry On Lunch'.
topics.firstPrinciples: A relational database stores data in tables (relations). Each table has a schema (column names + types). SQL queries declare WHAT you want (not HOW to get it) — the database engine figures out the optimal execution plan. Indexes make lookups O(log n) instead of O(n).
topics.bruteForce: Without SQL: load entire table into memory, loop through every row in code, apply filters manually — O(n) with no optimization. SQL's query planner uses indexes, statistics, and join algorithms to do this orders of magnitude faster.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use WHERE before GROUP BY to filter rows early (fewer rows to aggregate). Use indexes on columns in WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY. Use LIMIT to avoid fetching unnecessary rows. Use specific column names instead of SELECT * to reduce network transfer.
topics.whenToUse: SELECT: read/query data. WHERE: filter rows by condition. GROUP BY + aggregate: summarize groups. HAVING: filter groups (post-aggregation). ORDER BY: sort results. DISTINCT: remove duplicates. LIMIT/OFFSET: pagination.

examples.title: SELECT with WHERE — Filter Rows
examples.code:
-- Find all employees in Engineering with salary > 60000
SELECT id, name, salary, department
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Engineering'
  AND salary > 60000
ORDER BY salary DESC;
examples.tableData: {"type":"select","table":{"name":"employees","columns":["id","name","salary","department"],"rows":[[1,"Alice",85000,"Engineering"],[2,"Bob",55000,"Engineering"],[3,"Carol",92000,"Engineering"],[4,"Dave",70000,"Marketing"],[5,"Eve",78000,"Engineering"]]},"selectedColumns":["id","name","salary","department"],"whereCondition":"department='Engineering' AND salary > 60000","filteredRows":[0,2,4],"result":{"columns":["id","name","salary","department"],"rows":[[3,"Carol",92000,"Engineering"],[1,"Alice",85000,"Engineering"],[5,"Eve",78000,"Engineering"]]},"note":"Bob(55000) is Engineering but below threshold. Dave(70000) is not Engineering. ORDER BY salary DESC sorts highest first."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":2,"lineCode":"SELECT id, name, salary, department","variables":{"step":"Column selection"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Specify which columns to return. SELECT * returns all — bad practice in production (wastes bandwidth)."},{"line":3,"lineCode":"FROM employees","variables":{"rows":5},"phase":"FROM","annotation":"Start with full employees table: 5 rows."},{"line":4,"lineCode":"WHERE department = 'Engineering' AND salary > 60000","variables":{"passing":3,"filtered_out":2},"phase":"WHERE","annotation":"Filter: Bob(55k, fails salary) and Dave(Marketing, fails dept) excluded. 3 rows pass."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"ORDER BY salary DESC","variables":{"order":"Carol(92k) > Alice(85k) > Eve(78k)"},"phase":"ORDER","annotation":"Sort remaining 3 rows by salary descending. Carol ranks first."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["employees table\n5 rows"] --> B["WHERE department='Engineering'\n→ 4 rows"]
    B --> C["AND salary > 60000\n→ 3 rows"]
    C --> D["ORDER BY salary DESC"]
    D --> E["Result: Carol, Alice, Eve"]
examples.pseudocode:
QUERY execution order:
1. FROM employees      → load/reference the table
2. WHERE ...           → filter rows (happens BEFORE SELECT!)
3. SELECT id,name,...  → choose columns from filtered rows
4. ORDER BY salary DESC → sort result
5. LIMIT (if present)  → truncate

Note: SQL is DECLARATIVE — you say WHAT, DB decides HOW.
examples.explanation: WHERE filters rows BEFORE SELECT processes them. Logical execution order: FROM → WHERE → SELECT → ORDER BY (despite SELECT appearing first in syntax). This matters: you can't reference a SELECT alias in WHERE — use HAVING or a subquery instead. AND/OR operators combine conditions; AND requires both to be true.
examples.realWorldUse: E-commerce: 'find all orders over ₹1000 in the last 30 days'. Analytics: 'active users who logged in this week'. HR systems: 'engineers earning above median'. Every backend API endpoint that returns filtered data runs a WHERE clause underneath.

examples.title: GROUP BY + Aggregates — Summarize Groups
examples.code:
-- Headcount and average salary per department
SELECT
    department,
    COUNT(*)         AS headcount,
    AVG(salary)      AS avg_salary,
    MAX(salary)      AS max_salary,
    MIN(salary)      AS min_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"FROM employees","variables":{"total_rows":6},"phase":"FROM","annotation":"Start: 6 rows in employees table."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"GROUP BY department","variables":{"groups":"Engineering(4 rows), Marketing(2 rows)"},"phase":"GROUP","annotation":"Split rows into buckets by department value. Engineering bucket: Alice,Bob,Carol,Eve. Marketing: Dave,Frank."},{"line":2,"lineCode":"COUNT(*), AVG(salary), MAX(salary), MIN(salary)","variables":{"Engineering":"count=4,avg=77500,max=92000,min=55000","Marketing":"count=2,avg=67500,max=70000,min=65000"},"phase":"AGGREGATE","annotation":"Apply aggregate functions to EACH bucket independently. One output row per group."},{"line":9,"lineCode":"ORDER BY avg_salary DESC","variables":{"order":"Engineering(77500) > Marketing(67500)"},"phase":"ORDER","annotation":"Sort the 2 result rows by avg_salary. Engineering comes first."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["6 rows"] --> B["GROUP BY department"]
    B --> C["Engineering bucket\nAlice,Bob,Carol,Eve"]
    B --> D["Marketing bucket\nDave,Frank"]
    C --> E["COUNT=4\nAVG=77500\nMAX=92000"]
    D --> F["COUNT=2\nAVG=67500\nMAX=70000"]
    E --> G["2 result rows"]
    F --> G
examples.pseudocode:
GROUP BY mental model:
1. FROM employees → 6 rows
2. GROUP BY department → split into buckets
   [Engineering: row1,row2,row3,row5]
   [Marketing:   row4,row6]
3. For EACH bucket, compute aggregates:
   COUNT(*) = number of rows in bucket
   AVG(salary) = sum(salaries) / count
   MAX/MIN = extremes in bucket
4. Each bucket → 1 output row
5. ORDER BY avg_salary DESC

Rule: any column in SELECT must be in GROUP BY
      OR wrapped in an aggregate function.
examples.explanation: GROUP BY collapses multiple rows into one row per group. After grouping, you can only reference: (1) columns you grouped by, (2) aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN). Rule: every non-aggregate column in SELECT must appear in GROUP BY. Violation is a SQL error. Aggregates compute across each group independently.
examples.realWorldUse: Dashboard KPIs: sales by region, users by country, errors by service. Analytics: revenue per product category. Operations: average response time per endpoint. HR: headcount and salary bands per department. GROUP BY is the foundation of every BI report and analytics query.

examples.title: HAVING — Filter Groups After Aggregation
examples.code:
-- Departments with more than 2 employees
-- and average salary above 70000
SELECT
    department,
    COUNT(*)    AS headcount,
    AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2
   AND AVG(salary) > 70000
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;
examples.tableData: {"type":"select","table":{"name":"aggregated_groups","columns":["department","headcount","avg_salary"],"rows":[["Engineering",4,77500],["Marketing",2,67500],["HR",1,60000]]},"selectedColumns":["department","headcount","avg_salary"],"whereCondition":"COUNT(*) > 2 AND AVG(salary) > 70000","filteredRows":[0],"result":{"columns":["department","headcount","avg_salary"],"rows":[["Engineering",4,77500]]},"note":"Marketing fails COUNT(*) > 2 (only 2 employees). HR fails both. Only Engineering passes HAVING."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":7,"lineCode":"FROM employees GROUP BY department","variables":{"groups":"Engineering(4), Marketing(2), HR(1)"},"phase":"GROUP","annotation":"First: GROUP BY creates aggregated groups. 3 departments."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"HAVING COUNT(*) > 2 AND AVG(salary) > 70000","variables":{"Engineering":"count=4 ✓, avg=77500 ✓","Marketing":"count=2 ✗","HR":"count=1 ✗"},"phase":"HAVING","annotation":"HAVING filters the GROUP BY result. Marketing: 2 > 2 is false → excluded. HR: 1 > 2 is false → excluded."},{"line":1,"lineCode":"result","variables":{"result":"Engineering only"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Only Engineering survives HAVING. HAVING vs WHERE: WHERE filters ROWS (before grouping), HAVING filters GROUPS (after grouping)."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["FROM employees\n(all rows)"] --> B["WHERE (if any)\nfilter rows"]
    B --> C["GROUP BY department\n→ 3 groups"]
    C --> D["Compute aggregates\nper group"]
    D --> E["HAVING COUNT > 2\nAND AVG > 70000"]
    E --> F["Engineering ✓"]
    E --> G["Marketing ✗ (count=2)"]
    E --> H["HR ✗ (count=1)"]
    F --> I["Result: Engineering"]
examples.pseudocode:
WHERE  filters ROWS   → happens BEFORE GROUP BY
HAVING filters GROUPS → happens AFTER  GROUP BY

SELECT department, COUNT(*), AVG(salary)
FROM employees
[WHERE department != 'Temp']    ← filter ROWS first
GROUP BY department
[HAVING COUNT(*) > 2]           ← filter GROUPS after
ORDER BY ...

You CANNOT use aggregate functions in WHERE:
  WHERE COUNT(*) > 2   ← INVALID
  HAVING COUNT(*) > 2  ← VALID
examples.explanation: HAVING is like WHERE but for groups. The execution order: WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING. Use WHERE to filter rows early (cheaper — fewer rows to group). Use HAVING to filter on aggregate results. Common mistake: putting aggregate conditions in WHERE — that's a SQL error. HAVING can reference aliases defined in SELECT only in some databases (MySQL allows it, PostgreSQL doesn't always).
examples.realWorldUse: Find products with more than 100 reviews. Find cities where average order value exceeds ₹5000. Find services with error rate above 5%. Find users who have ordered more than 3 times. Any 'filter by summary statistic' query uses HAVING.

examples.title: DISTINCT and ORDER BY — Deduplication and Sorting
examples.code:
-- Unique departments, sorted alphabetically
SELECT DISTINCT department
FROM employees
ORDER BY department ASC;

-- Multi-column sort: department then salary
SELECT name, department, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY department ASC, salary DESC;
examples.tableData: {"type":"select","table":{"name":"employees","columns":["id","name","salary","department"],"rows":[[1,"Alice",85000,"Engineering"],[2,"Bob",55000,"Engineering"],[3,"Carol",92000,"Engineering"],[4,"Dave",70000,"Marketing"],[5,"Frank",65000,"Marketing"]]},"selectedColumns":["department"],"whereCondition":"DISTINCT","filteredRows":[0,3],"result":{"columns":["department"],"rows":[["Engineering"],["Marketing"]]},"note":"DISTINCT collapses 3 Engineering rows and 2 Marketing rows into 1 each. Result: 2 unique departments."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":2,"lineCode":"SELECT DISTINCT department","variables":{"raw":"Engineering,Engineering,Engineering,Marketing,Marketing"},"phase":"DISTINCT","annotation":"Without DISTINCT: 5 rows, 3 say Engineering, 2 say Marketing. DISTINCT removes duplicates."},{"line":3,"lineCode":"FROM employees ORDER BY department ASC","variables":{"result":"Engineering, Marketing"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"After DISTINCT: 2 unique values. ORDER BY sorts alphabetically: Engineering before Marketing."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"ORDER BY department ASC, salary DESC","variables":{"order":"group by dept first, then highest salary within dept"},"phase":"MULTI_SORT","annotation":"Multi-column sort: primary key = department (A→Z), secondary key = salary (high→low within each dept). Tie-breaking."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A["5 rows"] --> B["SELECT department"]
    B --> C["Engineering\nEngineering\nEngineering\nMarketing\nMarketing"]
    C --> D["DISTINCT"]
    D --> E["Engineering\nMarketing"]
    E --> F["ORDER BY ASC"]
    F --> G["Engineering\nMarketing"]
examples.pseudocode:
DISTINCT: remove duplicate result rows
  SELECT DISTINCT col → unique values only
  Cost: O(n log n) — sort + deduplicate
  Alternative: GROUP BY col (same effect)

ORDER BY col1 ASC, col2 DESC:
  PRIMARY sort: col1 ascending
  SECONDARY sort: col2 descending (within tied col1)
  NULL values: appear first (ASC) or last (DESC) — varies by DB
  No ORDER BY = result order is NOT guaranteed!
examples.explanation: DISTINCT operates on the entire selected row — not just one column. ORDER BY allows multiple sort keys: the first key is primary, subsequent keys break ties. Without ORDER BY, SQL makes NO guarantee about result order — even the same query can return rows in different order on different runs. Always add ORDER BY when order matters (pagination, reports).
examples.realWorldUse: DISTINCT: unique countries in user base. Unique error codes in logs. Unique product categories. ORDER BY: paginated product lists sorted by price. Leader board by score. Support tickets sorted by priority then date. LIMIT + ORDER BY = pagination.

examples.title: Aggregate Functions — COUNT, SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN
examples.code:
-- Full aggregate summary
SELECT
    COUNT(*)              AS total_employees,
    COUNT(bonus)          AS employees_with_bonus,   -- NULLs excluded
    SUM(salary)           AS total_payroll,
    AVG(salary)           AS avg_salary,
    MAX(salary)           AS highest_salary,
    MIN(salary)           AS lowest_salary,
    ROUND(AVG(salary), 2) AS avg_rounded
FROM employees
WHERE department = 'Engineering';
examples.tableData: {"type":"select","table":{"name":"employees","columns":["id","name","salary","bonus","department"],"rows":[[1,"Alice",85000,5000,"Engineering"],[2,"Bob",55000,null,"Engineering"],[3,"Carol",92000,8000,"Engineering"],[4,"Eve",78000,null,"Engineering"]]},"selectedColumns":["salary","bonus"],"whereCondition":"department='Engineering'","filteredRows":[0,1,2,3],"result":{"columns":["total_employees","employees_with_bonus","total_payroll","avg_salary","highest_salary","lowest_salary"],"rows":[[4,2,310000,77500,92000,55000]]},"note":"COUNT(*) counts all rows (4). COUNT(bonus) counts only non-NULL bonus values (2). NULL rows are invisible to most aggregate functions."}
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"COUNT(*) vs COUNT(bonus)","variables":{"COUNT_star":4,"COUNT_bonus":2},"phase":"COUNT","annotation":"COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs. COUNT(column) counts only non-NULL values. Bob and Eve have NULL bonus → COUNT(bonus)=2."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"SUM(salary)","variables":{"sum":"85000+55000+92000+78000=310000"},"phase":"SUM","annotation":"SUM adds all salary values. SUM ignores NULLs. SUM(NULL) = 0 only if no other values; if all NULL → result is NULL."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"AVG(salary)","variables":{"avg":"310000/4=77500"},"phase":"AVG","annotation":"AVG = SUM/COUNT (non-NULL). AVG also ignores NULLs in the denominator — AVG([1,NULL,3]) = (1+3)/2 = 2, not 4/3."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["4 Engineering rows"] --> B["COUNT(*) = 4"]
    A --> C["COUNT(bonus) = 2 (NULLs skipped)"]
    A --> D["SUM(salary) = 310000"]
    A --> E["AVG = 310000/4 = 77500"]
    A --> F["MAX = 92000 (Carol)"]
    A --> G["MIN = 55000 (Bob)"]
examples.pseudocode:
Aggregate function rules:
  COUNT(*) = count ALL rows (NULLs included)
  COUNT(col) = count non-NULL values only
  SUM(col) = add all non-NULL values
  AVG(col) = SUM(col) / COUNT(col)  [ignores NULLs]
  MAX/MIN(col) = extreme non-NULL value

All aggregates ignore NULL values (except COUNT(*))
All aggregates over an empty set return NULL (except COUNT → 0)

USEFUL: COALESCE(AVG(col), 0) to handle NULL result
examples.explanation: The NULL behavior of aggregates is critical: COUNT(*) includes NULLs, all others exclude them. AVG automatically adjusts its denominator — it divides by the count of non-NULL values, not the total row count. This means AVG([10, NULL, 20]) = 15, not 10. ROUND(value, decimals) formats the output. These rules matter for billing, payroll, and analytics systems.
examples.realWorldUse: Payroll: total salary budget. E-commerce: average order value, maximum order size. Analytics: active users count (COUNT DISTINCT), total revenue (SUM). SLA monitoring: average response time, maximum latency. Inventory: total stock value, minimum reorder level.

problems.title: Recyclable and Low Fat Products
problems.description:
Find the ids of products that are both low fat and recyclable.

**Table: Products**
| Column | Type |
|--------|------|
| product_id | int |
| low_fats | enum('Y','N') |
| recyclable | enum('Y','N') |

**Example:**
Input: Products = [[0,'Y','N'],[1,'Y','Y'],[2,'N','Y'],[3,'Y','Y'],[4,'N','N']]
Output: [[1],[3]]
problems.hint1: You need both conditions to be true simultaneously. What operator joins two conditions where both must be true?
problems.hint2: Use AND to combine two WHERE conditions: low_fats = 'Y' AND recyclable = 'Y'.
problems.hint3: SELECT product_id FROM Products WHERE low_fats = 'Y' AND recyclable = 'Y';
problems.starterCode:
SELECT product_id
FROM Products
-- Your WHERE condition here
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT product_id
FROM Products
WHERE low_fats = 'Y'
  AND recyclable = 'Y';
problems.constraints: The number of rows in Products will be in range [1, 100].
problems.editorial: Simple AND filter: WHERE low_fats = 'Y' AND recyclable = 'Y'. Both conditions must be true. O(n) table scan; with indexes on both columns O(log n) per condition.

problems.title: Find Customer Referee
problems.description:
Find the names of customers NOT referred by customer with id = 2.

**Table: Customer**
| Column | Type |
|--------|------|
| id | int |
| name | varchar |
| referee_id | int (nullable) |

**Example:**
Input: Customer = [[1,'Will',null],[2,'Jane',null],[3,'Alex',2],[4,'Bill',null],[5,'Zack',1],[6,'Mark',2]]
Output: [['Will'],['Jane'],['Bill'],['Zack']]
problems.hint1: referee_id can be NULL. Does `referee_id != 2` include NULL rows? Think about how SQL handles NULL comparisons.
problems.hint2: NULL comparisons: NULL != 2 is NULL (not TRUE). Use IS NULL to explicitly check for NULL. Combine with OR.
problems.hint3: WHERE referee_id != 2 OR referee_id IS NULL — this includes customers with no referee AND customers with a different referee.
problems.starterCode:
SELECT name
FROM Customer
-- Handle the NULL case!
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT name
FROM Customer
WHERE referee_id != 2
   OR referee_id IS NULL;
problems.editorial: Key trap: NULL != 2 evaluates to NULL (unknown), not TRUE — so WHERE referee_id != 2 silently drops NULL rows. Fix: add OR referee_id IS NULL. Alternative: WHERE COALESCE(referee_id, 0) != 2.

problems.title: Big Countries
problems.description:
A country is big if it has an area of at least 3 million km² or population of at least 25 million. Find the name, population, and area of big countries.

**Table: World**
| Column | Type |
|--------|------|
| name | varchar |
| continent | varchar |
| area | int |
| population | int |
| gdp | bigint |

**Example:**
Input: World = [['Afghanistan','Asia',652230,25500100,20343000000],['Albania','Europe',28748,2831741,12960000000]]
Output: [['Afghanistan',25500100,652230]]
problems.hint1: Use OR to match countries satisfying EITHER condition (area OR population threshold).
problems.hint3: SELECT name, population, area FROM World WHERE area >= 3000000 OR population >= 25000000;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT name, population, area
FROM World
-- Your WHERE condition here
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT name, population, area
FROM World
WHERE area >= 3000000
   OR population >= 25000000;
problems.editorial: OR: at least one condition must be true. AND requires both. Here area OR population — either threshold qualifies a country.

problems.title: Department Highest Salary
problems.description:
Find employees who have the highest salary in their department.

**Table: Employee**
| id | name | salary | departmentId |

**Table: Department**
| id | name |

**Example:**
Employee: [[1,'Joe',70000,1],[2,'Jim',90000,1],[3,'Henry',80000,2],[4,'Sam',60000,2],[5,'Max',90000,1]]
Department: [[1,'IT'],[2,'Sales']]
Output: [['IT','Jim',90000],['IT','Max',90000],['Sales','Henry',80000]]
problems.hint1: You need the maximum salary per department. How do you get the max salary for each department?
problems.hint2: Use GROUP BY departmentId to get MAX(salary) per department. Then join Employee back to find all employees with that salary.
problems.hint3: JOIN Employee with a subquery: SELECT departmentId, MAX(salary) FROM Employee GROUP BY departmentId. Match employee rows where their salary equals the department max.
problems.starterCode:
SELECT d.name AS Department, e.name AS Employee, e.salary AS Salary
FROM Employee e
JOIN Department d ON e.departmentId = d.id
-- Add condition to filter highest earners per department
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT d.name AS Department, e.name AS Employee, e.salary AS Salary
FROM Employee e
JOIN Department d ON e.departmentId = d.id
WHERE (e.departmentId, e.salary) IN (
    SELECT departmentId, MAX(salary)
    FROM Employee
    GROUP BY departmentId
);
problems.editorial: Subquery gets (departmentId, maxSalary) pairs. Outer query filters employees matching those pairs. Multiple employees can tie for max. Alternative: window function RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY departmentId ORDER BY salary DESC) = 1.

problems.title: Consecutive Numbers
problems.description:
Find all numbers that appear at least three times consecutively in the Logs table.

**Table: Logs**
| id (auto-increment) | num |

**Example:**
Input: Logs = [[1,1],[2,1],[3,1],[4,2],[5,1],[6,2],[7,2]]
Output: [[1]] — only 1 appears three times in a row (rows 1,2,3).
problems.hint1: You need to compare a row with the row 1 ahead and 2 ahead. How can you reference the same table three times?
problems.hint2: Self-join: JOIN Logs l1, Logs l2, Logs l3 where l1.id+1=l2.id AND l2.id+1=l3.id. Then check if all three nums match.
problems.hint3: SELECT DISTINCT l1.num FROM Logs l1 JOIN Logs l2 ON l1.id+1=l2.id JOIN Logs l3 ON l2.id+1=l3.id WHERE l1.num=l2.num AND l2.num=l3.num.
problems.starterCode:
SELECT DISTINCT l1.num AS ConsecutiveNums
FROM Logs l1, Logs l2, Logs l3
-- Your join conditions and WHERE clause
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT DISTINCT l1.num AS ConsecutiveNums
FROM Logs l1
JOIN Logs l2 ON l1.id + 1 = l2.id
JOIN Logs l3 ON l2.id + 1 = l3.id
WHERE l1.num = l2.num
  AND l2.num = l3.num;
problems.editorial: Self-join: reference the same table three times with different aliases. id+1 links consecutive rows. All three nums must match. DISTINCT removes duplicates if a number appears consecutively in multiple places.

problems.title: Nth Highest Salary
problems.description:
Write a SQL function to get the Nth highest salary from the Employee table. If there is no Nth highest salary, return null.

**Example:**
Employee = [[1,100],[2,200],[3,300]] → getNthHighestSalary(2) → 200
Employee = [[1,100]] → getNthHighestSalary(2) → null
problems.hint1: Use ORDER BY salary DESC to rank. How do you skip N-1 salaries and take just the Nth?
problems.hint2: LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1 returns 1 row starting from position N-1 (0-indexed). But handle duplicates with DISTINCT.
problems.hint3: SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM Employee ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1. Wrap in a function to return NULL when not found.
problems.starterCode:
CREATE FUNCTION getNthHighestSalary(N INT) RETURNS INT
BEGIN
  -- Your SQL here
END
problems.editorial: LIMIT 1 OFFSET N-1: skip N-1 rows, return 1. DISTINCT handles duplicate salaries (if two people earn 200, that's still the 2nd-highest salary level). Returns NULL automatically when OFFSET exceeds result count.

problems.title: Game Play Analysis I
problems.description:
Find the first login date for each player.

**Table: Activity**
| player_id | device_id | event_date | games_played |
(player_id, event_date) is the primary key

**Example:**
Activity = [[1,2,'2016-03-01',5],[1,2,'2016-05-02',6],[2,3,'2017-06-25',1],[3,1,'2016-03-02',0],[3,4,'2018-07-03',5]]
Output: [[1,'2016-03-01'],[2,'2017-06-25'],[3,'2016-03-02']]
problems.hint1: You need the minimum (earliest) event_date per player. What aggregate function finds the minimum?
problems.hint2: GROUP BY player_id to get one row per player, MIN(event_date) to get the earliest date.
problems.hint3: SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date) AS first_login FROM Activity GROUP BY player_id;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT player_id, MIN(event_date) AS first_login
FROM Activity
-- GROUP BY player
problems.editorial: GROUP BY player_id collapses all activity rows per player into one. MIN(event_date) selects the earliest date within each player's group. O(n) scan.

problems.title: Classes with More than 5 Students
problems.description:
Find all classes that have at least 5 students.

**Table: Courses**
| student | class |

**Example:**
Courses = [['A','Math'],['B','English'],['C','Math'],['D','Biology'],['E','Math'],['F','Computer'],['G','Math'],['H','Math'],['I','Math']]
Output: [['Math']]
problems.hint1: Count students per class using GROUP BY. Then filter classes with >= 5 students. Use WHERE or HAVING?
problems.hint2: You're filtering by an aggregate (COUNT), so use HAVING — not WHERE.
problems.hint3: SELECT class FROM Courses GROUP BY class HAVING COUNT(student) >= 5;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT class
FROM Courses
-- GROUP and filter here
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT class
FROM Courses
GROUP BY class
HAVING COUNT(student) >= 5;
problems.editorial: GROUP BY class, then HAVING COUNT >= 5. HAVING filters AFTER grouping — the only way to filter by aggregate values.

problems.title: Employees Earning More than Managers
problems.description:
Find employees who earn more than their managers.

**Table: Employee**
| id | name | salary | managerId |

**Example:**
Employee = [[1,'Joe',70000,3],[2,'Henry',80000,4],[3,'Sam',60000,null],[4,'Max',90000,null]]
Output: [['Joe']] — Joe(70000) earns more than Sam(60000) his manager.
problems.hint1: You need to compare each employee's salary with their manager's salary. Both are in the same table. How do you join a table to itself?
problems.hint2: Self-join Employee e JOIN Employee m ON e.managerId = m.id. This links each employee row to their manager row.
problems.hint3: SELECT e.name FROM Employee e JOIN Employee m ON e.managerId = m.id WHERE e.salary > m.salary;
problems.starterCode:
SELECT e.name AS Employee
FROM Employee e
-- Self join to get manager, then filter
problems.solutionCode:
SELECT e.name AS Employee
FROM Employee e
JOIN Employee m ON e.managerId = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;
problems.editorial: Self-join: JOIN Employee to itself with different aliases (e=employee, m=manager). This pairs each employee with their manager on the same row. WHERE e.salary > m.salary filters for the interesting cases.

problems.title: Delete Duplicate Emails
problems.description:
Delete all duplicate emails, keeping only the one with the smallest id for each email.

**Table: Person**
| id | email |

**Example:**
Person = [[1,'john@example.com'],[2,'bob@example.com'],[3,'john@example.com']]
After delete: Person = [[1,'john@example.com'],[2,'bob@example.com']]
problems.hint1: Which rows should be deleted? Those where the same email exists with a SMALLER id. How do you compare a row to another row with the same email?
problems.hint2: Self-join: p1 JOIN p2 ON p1.email = p2.email AND p1.id > p2.id. Rows where p1.id > p2.id are duplicates (a smaller id exists for the same email).
problems.hint3: DELETE p1 FROM Person p1 JOIN Person p2 ON p1.email = p2.email AND p1.id > p2.id;
problems.starterCode:
-- Delete duplicates keeping lowest id
DELETE p1
FROM Person p1
-- Join and condition here
problems.solutionCode:
DELETE p1
FROM Person p1
JOIN Person p2
  ON p1.email = p2.email
  AND p1.id > p2.id;
problems.editorial: Self-join pairs duplicates: p1 and p2 share the same email, p1 has a larger id (so p1 is the duplicate to delete). DELETE p1 removes all such rows. Non-duplicate rows have no p2 match → not deleted.
